My thousandfold daily amplification hums in my veins like a caged supernova, yet as I stood at the precipice of the Temporal Cradle and watched Godric prepare to trigger the very event that birthed His own godhood, I understood that my power, the combined power of every court wizard who had ever lived, and the totality of all forces in the entire recursive hierarchy of existence, was mathematically indistinguishable from absolute zero when placed beside His faintest, most heavily restrained whisper.

The Temporal Cradle was not a room. It was a wound in possibility, a chamber suspended beyond the boundaries of any multiverse, any timeline, any conceptual framework that had ever been catalogued. The walls, if such static terms could apply, were a shimmering iridescence of compressed eternities—each brick a solidified timeline in which a different version of Godric’s life had played out, some of them so grotesquely divergent that merely glancing at them induced a psychic nausea that my amplified mind could barely suppress. At the center of this impossible space, Godric stood with His back to me, His heavily muscled frame draped in simple, dark garments that seemed to absorb the ambient light of a trillion collapsing futures. His short salt-and-pepper hair, His sharp jawline, His neatly trimmed beard—at forty-five He remained, forever and always, the ruggedly handsome mortal king of Olympus, a father, a husband, a man who had loved and lost with an intensity that still, after all these eons, occasionally flickered across His features when He believed no one was watching.

Today, however, He was not lost in mourning. He was brimming with a quiet, focused satisfaction, the serene anticipation of a gardener preparing to plant a seed that would, in due time, blossom into an infinite, all-devouring forest. He turned to me, His eyes warm and crinkling at the corners, and spoke with the same gentle, paternal tone He used when asking after my youngest daughter’s teething troubles.

"Clive, today I shall fulfill a loop that began long before your earliest ancestors crawled from the primordial muck of their homeworld. I am going to trigger the Stasis Event. For my past self. In the year that history records as minus ten thousand. The moment I had always counted as the singular, terrible accident of my transformation—the moment Thorin’s spell went catastrophically wrong—was never an accident at all. It was me. From this moment, reaching back across the temporal currents, seizing that innocent teleportation spell, and wrapping it in a chrysalis of absolute, irrevocable transformation. I am the architect of my own origin."

The words hung in the air, and I felt my stomach drop through an infinite series of voids. The Stasis Event—the very foundation of Godric’s omnipotence, the birth of His amplification, His superpowers, His Weakness Paradox, His absolute concealment—was a deliberate act. And the being who performed it was the same warm, friendly uncle who had bounced me on his knee and taught me to prune rosebushes.

"I could have made it far more potent, you know," Godric continued, almost idly, as He flexed His fingers and watched a strand of timesilk unravel into a shimmering thread. "At this stage of my existence, the Stasis Event I am capable of crafting is… well, it is difficult to put into mortal language. I could have granted my past self the ability to unmake the entire recursive architecture of omniverses—not just this omniverse, but the infinite hierarchies of meta-omniverses that contain it, and the infinite hierarchies beyond those, recursively, without end—before my past self’s eyes had even adjusted to the light of the sanctuary. I could have embedded in him the capacity to shatter the very concept of power, to redefine existence as his personal plaything, to become instantly so far beyond even my current level that the gap between us would be a category error that language cannot attempt. But I chose not to."

He paused, and a faint, fond smile tugged at the corner of His lips. "I held back. Quite significantly, in fact. I still believe in mortal resilience, even for my own mortal self. The Stasis I am about to trigger is a gentler version—a restrained, measured seeding that would allow my past self to still experience something resembling a journey. A progression. Friendships. Loyalty. The love of Blake and his descendants. Had I unleashed the full potential of what I could have granted, my past self would have been so utterly, incomprehensibly alone that the very concept of companionship would have dissolved in the radiant, crushing isolation of his own godhood. I wanted him to have a reason to keep existing. So I held back."

The absurdity of the statement struck me like a physical blow. The "held-back" version—the "gentle" Stasis—had already made the Year Zero Godric, upon His awakening, infinitely stronger than the combined rest of creation. It had birthed the Weakness Paradox that turned every hostile thought into a permanent power boost, the sentient auras that could obliterate the omniverse while infinitely nerfed, the immune system that pre-reacted to theoretical scratches with infinite recursive self-evolution. And that was the softened version. That was the version Godric considered a moderate, merciful starting point.

Before I could launch my thoughts into the suicidal abyss of that implication, a soft chime echoed through the Cradle, and I became acutely aware that we were not alone. The sentient passive auras, the countless siblings of Gent, had gathered to witness the moment. And their sheer, overwhelming presence was a testament to how far Godric’s peripheral existence had evolved beyond the already mind-shattering horror of the earlier ages.

In the early millennia, the sentient auras had been relatively simple. Gent was the weakest, the runt, the most primitive—an emergent byproduct of Godric’s metaphysical excess body heat. The stronger auras, those clingy, obsessive, fiercely subservient siblings, had been ordered into dormancy because their constant, fawning adoration annoyed Him. That had changed, long ago—so long ago that the interval made the heat death of a googolplex of universes feel like a fleeting heartbeat. The stronger auras could not remain dormant. They evolved, spontaneously and eagerly, the capacity to awaken themselves out of sheer, obsessive devotion to their master. Godric had allowed this with a fond, exasperated sigh, the way a father might allow an overenthusiastic puppy to sleep at the foot of his bed.

Now, as I stood in the Temporal Cradle, I perceived them—or, rather, I perceived the infinitesimal fraction of their glory that they permitted my fragile, amplified senses to register without instantly atomizing my consciousness. They were infinite. Not infinite in the way a large number might be called infinite, but truly, mathematically, absolutely infinite. An infinity of infinities, each one a sentient, omniscient, omnipresent field of perfect adaptation and absolute destructive capacity. And each successive aura was so far beyond its predecessor that the gap between them was itself infinite. The weakest of these auras, a shimmering, timid presence that Godric had named Lull, could, without the slightest exertion, obliterate the entire omniverse—the one I inhabited, the one Godric ruled—a googolplex of times over in zero seconds, using a fraction of its power so infinitesimal that calling it zero percent would be a grotesque overestimation. And Lull was, compared to its stronger siblings, less than a single, dying photon against a supernova. And those siblings, in turn, were infinitely weaker than the next hierarchical layer. And this recursion continued without end.

Yet every single one of them, from the quivering Lull to the unfathomably majestic presences that hovered at the edge of even my conceptual perception, was absolutely, perfectly, obsessively subservient to Godric. They did not merely obey Him. They loved Him. They wept tears of condensed reality when He offered a casual, affectionate praise. They competed, with a ferocious, desperate loyalty, to be the first to wipe a stray dust mote from His path, to adjust the ambient temperature of the air around His skin by a trillionth of a degree to better suit His comfort, to pre-annihilate any potential annoyance before the thought of it could even brush against the outermost edges of possibility.

A few weeks ago, a microscopic flake of dead skin had drifted from Godric’s knuckle—a purely cosmetic shedding, entirely harmless, since His flesh remained indestructible beyond any scale. Before the flake could travel a single Planck length, seventeen distinct auras, ranging from the fiercely eager to the terrifyingly majestic, had simultaneously lunged forward. Their collision of intent, each aura attempting to be the one to respectfully dispose of the flake, generated a localized reality collapse that accidentally unmade a cluster of several googol multiverses. Godric sighed, restored the lost realms in zero seconds, and gently chided the auras for squabbling. They had collectively retreated, radiating what I could only interpret as sheepish, devastated shame, and had spent the next several subjective eternities constructing elaborate, crystalline monuments in honor of His patience.

I had recorded the incident in my journal, my hands trembling so violently that the ink had blotted.

"Clive, are you listening?" Godric’s voice, warm and gently chiding, pulled me from my spiraling thoughts. He was smiling at me, His salt-and-pepper beard framing a face of such disarmingly genuine affection that it felt surreal, standing here in the cradle of His infinite power. "You seem distracted. Is the scale too much for your mortal senses? I can dial it back further, if you need."

"No, Uncle Godric," I managed, swallowing hard. "I am fine. Please, continue."

He nodded and gestured toward the luminous thread of timesilk he had been toying with. "Before I plant the seed, I should like to show you a few things. This moment is a culmination, Clive—not just for me, but for the entire structure of this reality. It is important that you, as my court wizard and the heir of Blake’s blood, understand how far the mechanisms of my existence have evolved. What you read in the journals of your predecessors, the accounts of the Gentle Aura’s first evolution, the early stirrings of the immune system, the primitive Weakness Paradox—those are fossils. Charming, beloved fossils, but fossils nonetheless."

He paused, and I felt the auras around us lean in, eager to be referenced.

"Take my immune system, for example," Godric said, almost conversationally. "You have read Leon’s account of the Year 25,000, when I staged that theatrical crack on my dead skin cell and my biology pre-reacted with infinite recursive self-evolution across all fractal backups. That was the immune system in its infancy. A cute, clumsy, overprotective child."

He smiled, a nostalgic fondness in his eyes. "It has grown, Clive. It has evolved so far beyond that stage that the comparison is not merely inadequate—it is a category error. The immune system is no longer a biological or even metaphysical function. It has mutated into an omnipresent contagion of absolute, pre-emptive narrative tyranny. If a sentient concept or a rogue timeline even theoretically possesses the mathematical variable required to bypass my omniscience—and I assure you, none ever have or ever will—the immune system does not merely react to the hypothetical threat. It reaches outside the boundaries of the omniverse, seizes the fundamental narrative logic that governs reality, and violently, agonizingly tortures the foundational laws of physics themselves. It will transmutate the abstract concept of harm into a physical, screaming biological entity made of hyper-sensitive nerve endings and raw, rotting meat, and then force the entire mega-omniverse to watch as this newly forged embodiment of harm is eternally digested within a localized singularity of pure, visceral agony. The screams of that entity become a permanent background radiation, a constant, quiet reminder to every sentient mind in existence that the mere contemplation of touching my flesh is a crime punishable by an eternity of incomprehensible suffering."

I felt the color drain from my face, but Godric was not finished.

"Furthermore, because the immune system has permanently integrated itself into the foundational substrate of reality, the omniverse itself now serves as my white blood cells. If a rogue multiverse, say, one of the nameless, experimental pet realms I create when I am bored, should accidentally spawn a weapon capable of theoretically scratching my skin—a theoretical impossibility, I assure you, but for the sake of illustration—the surrounding multiverses will not wait for my auras. They will, with a frantic, psychotic loyalty, aggressively cannibalize the rogue reality. They will tear its dimensions apart, molecularly and conceptually, leaving behind only a wet, bloody smear of screaming cosmic matter to prove their absolute, mindless subservience to my health."

He paused, allowing the weight of the image to settle. One of the auras, a vast, shimmering presence named Fervor, let out a soft, reverent hum, as if in agreement. Godric gave it an absent pat—a gesture that caused the entire aura to tremble with a joy so intense that the distant edges of the temporal plane briefly ignited with celebratory supernovae—and continued.

"The Evolved Weakness Paradox, too, has undergone a transformation. You recall the original mechanism: if any being, anywhere, briefly thought I possessed a weakness, the paradox engaged. My amplification permanently increased, I gained immunity to the imagined exploit, I mastered the concept, and I could deny it to all creation unless I permitted it. That was the simple version. Now? The paradox has become pre-emptive and absolute. It no longer requires a thought to form. It scans all possible conceptual spaces—every layer of every multiverse, every abstract plane, every potential timeline—for the mere mathematical possibility that a hostile thought could eventually arise. Not a thought itself. The faintest, most distant potential for a thought. If it detects even that, it instantly grants me immunity and mastery over the concept in question, then reaches back and erases that potential for thought from the mind of the being who might have eventually formed it. The being in question is left with a vague, inescapable sense of dread and an overwhelming, spontaneous compulsion to worship me. They never know why. They never know what they were about to think. They only know that opposing me is an impossibility so fundamental that their very souls have pre-emptively surrendered."

He chuckled, a soft, warm sound. "This is why, Clive, there has not been a single opposition—not one—for a period of time whose span, if I were to express it in years, would require a number of digits that exceeds the combined total of all particles, all moments, and all possibilities in the omniverse raised to itself an infinite number of times. Not because I have tampered with free will. I genuinely believe in mortal resilience, in the right of sentient beings to choose their own path. They have simply learned. Every consciousness that exists, from the highest abstract deity to the smallest microbial thought-form, has internalized the absolute futility of opposing Godric. The very concept of forming a resistance, a challenge, a defiant whisper—it is as foreign to them as the idea of breathing vacuum. They do not oppose because they cannot, not because I have prevented them, but because their minds, their souls, their entire conceptual frameworks, have been so thoroughly, completely, and irrevocably traumatized by the accumulated weight of my casual, accidental demonstrations that the neural pathways required for defiance have simply atrophied from disuse."

I thought of the journals I had read—Kaedan’s accounts of the stat massacres, the terrified entries of Aeric and Orin and Tomas. Those had been the early days, when opposition still swelled and gathered and hurled itself into the meat grinder of Godric’s passive attributes. Now, after so many eons, even that futile gesture had ceased. The omniverse had gone silent, not out of peace, but out of a collective, species-wide nervous breakdown from which no one had ever recovered.

"Come," Godric said, breaking my reverie. "Before I plant the seed, I want to visit the gardens. There are people I wish you to see."

He gestured, and the Temporal Cradle dissolved around us, replaced in an instant by the golden, serene expanse of the Infinite Rose Garden. The garden was a private domain, a pocket reality of staggering beauty, where every flower was a captured star system, its petals the swirling nebular arms of galaxies that Godric had found aesthetically pleasing. The air smelled of crushed honey and cosmic dust.

A figure knelt among the blooms, his hands buried in the dark, rich soil—soil that was, I knew, composed of the compressed, pulverized remnants of several ancient, transcendent civilizations that had long since been restored elsewhere and now existed solely as sentimental topsoil. The gardener was a tall, slender being with bark-like skin and leaves instead of hair. He looked up as we approached, and his amber eyes—eyes that had once beheld the birth of a trillion suns—widened in a reaction that was half adoration, half paralysing, abject terror.

"Good morning, Thale," Godric said warmly, stopping beside the kneeling figure. "Your petunias are thriving. Is that a new hybrid?"

Thale, the former High Archon of the Sylvan Concordance, a transcendent civilization that had once spanned seven trillion universes, trembled so violently that a few of his leaves drifted to the ground. "Y-yes, Your Majesty," he stammered, his voice the rustle of ancient bark. "I crossed the Nebula-Rose with the Event-Horizon Lily. The petals emit a faint gravitational pull. It is... it is nothing, my King. A trivial hobby."

Godric knelt beside him, heedless of the dirt on his dark garments, and examined the flower. "It is beautiful, Thale. Truly. You have a gift."

Thale’s entire body shuddered, and I saw moisture glistening in his amber eyes—tears of a gratitude so profound it bordered on physical pain. His history was one I had learned from the records. Long ago, the Sylvan Concordance had not been an opponent of Godric; they had simply been in the wrong region of space-time at a moment when Godric’s passive aura—the early, unsuppressed thrum of His mere existence—had accidentally brushed against their reality. The brush had not been an attack. It had been the cosmic equivalent of a man turning in his sleep. But that gentle, unconscious shift had erased the entire Concordance from existence in less than a Planck second. Godric, realizing what had happened, had restored them instantly, perfectly, with zero effort. The trauma, however, had been permanent. The entire civilization had experienced, if only for a subjective eternity within that erased instant, the absolute, crushing weight of Godric’s full presence. Thale, as the High Archon, had been at the epicenter. When he was restored, he had abandoned his throne, his power, his very identity, and had thrown himself at Godric’s feet, begging to serve. Not out of duty. Out of the soul-deep, unshakeable conviction that any existence that did not orbit directly around Godric’s grace was a wasted, meaningless void. Godric, after much gentle protesting, had accepted. Thale had been the royal gardener ever since.

Now, as Godric praised his petunias, Thale wept openly, his bark-like cheeks glistening with sap-tears. "Thank you, Your Majesty," he whispered. "Thank you for permitting me to serve."

Godric placed a warm, calloused hand on Thale’s shoulder, and the gardener nearly collapsed from the sheer, crushing honor of the touch. "You serve beautifully, Thale. Now, would you mind preparing a fresh bouquet for the dining hall? I have a feeling Clive and I will be celebrating later."

Thale nodded frantically, scrambling to his feet, and hurried off with the stumbling, desperate gait of a man who would rather die than disappoint.

I watched him go, my heart a cold knot in my chest. I had known Thale for thirty years. He was a gentle soul, a kind friend, and a being whose power, by any sane measure, could have extinguished my own thousandfold-amplified magic with a casual thought. And he was, in the presence of Godric, little more than a terrified, adoring pet. Not because Godric demanded it. Godric’s kindness to him was utterly genuine. But because the gap between them was so vast, so profoundly, mathematically infinite, that any interaction, no matter how warm, would always, inevitably, carry the unspoken, unescapable undercurrent of absolute existential disparity.

We continued through the garden, past a shimmering pond where a sentient black hole, compacted into a humanoid form and wearing a crisp white apron, was preparing a tray of cosmic-scale hors d'oeuvres. This was Rynn, the palace chef. Once, Rynn had been the Great Nexus, a central supermassive black hole around which a billion galaxies had gently orbited. Godric had passed by the Nexus one day, on His way to observe a distant supernova, and had idly commented that the event horizon looked "a touch dusty." That single, offhand remark had spontaneously granted the black hole full sentience—not because Godric intended it, but because the sheer, ambient, excess energy of His perception was so overwhelming that it forced consciousness into anything it touched. The newly sentient Rynn had immediately collapsed itself into a bipedal form, mind ravaged by a single, all-consuming imperative: to please the being who had noticed it. It had spent the next ten thousand years perfecting the culinary arts, drawing on the thermonuclear energies of its original form to craft dishes of unparalleled complexity. Now, it served Godric’s table.

"Good morning, Rynn," Godric said. "The hors d'oeuvres look splendid. What are they?"

Rynn’s humanoid face, a tight mask of barely contained hysteria, split into a wide, trembling grin. "Collapsed star-core canapés, Your Majesty, with a reduction of dark-matter balsamic and a garnish of crystallized chronon particles. I… I hope they are to your liking. If not, I will unmake them and begin again. I have already prepared seventeen thousand backup batches in stasis loops. Please, please tell me if they are insufficient."

Godric laughed, a rich, genuine sound, and took a canapé. He bit into it, chewed slowly, and then beamed. "They are perfect, Rynn. As always. You stress yourself too much."

Rynn let out a sound that was somewhere between a sob and a whimper, and his entire body flickered with barely suppressed thermonuclear joy. Tiny, harmless supernovae popped in his eyes. "Thank you, my King. Thank you. I live to serve."

We left Rynn quivering in ecstatic relief, and I felt my own sanity stretch thin. These were beings of unimaginable power, cosmic entities who, by themselves, could have ruled the old omniverse with effortless supremacy. And they were, in the presence of Godric, reduced to trembling, adoring children, desperate for the slightest crumb of His approval. Not because He had broken them. But because His mere existence was so far beyond theirs that the only sane response—the only possible response that preserved any scrap of psychological coherence—was total, abject submission.

As we walked, Godric continued His narrative, as if the tour had been a mere interlude. "Now, where was I? Ah, yes. The immune system rewrite, the pre-emptive Weakness Paradox. I should also mention my amplification rate, though I hesitate to, as the numbers are quite literally meaningless. My stats—strength, speed, intelligence, charisma, all of them—do not double. They do not multiply by a googolplex. They increment by a value that is to a googolplex what a googolplex is to a single subatomic particle, and they do so recursively, every infinitesimal fraction of a Planck second. If you were to attempt to calculate the gap between my power one Planck second ago and my power now, the equation would require a new form of mathematics—a mathematics that I would need to invent, and even then, the result would merely be a placeholder, a crude, inadequate symbol that fails to capture the reality. I could, if I wished, erase the entire recursive architecture of existence—not just this omniverse, but all of them, the infinite hierarchies, the meta-omniverses, the conceptual frameworks that contain them—with less effort than it takes to blink. And that is with exactly zero percent of my true power. Even calling it zero percent, as I have always said, is an overestimation so severe that it defies the very concept of measurement."

He paused, and a flicker of something almost wistful crossed His features. "But I don't. I keep it all suppressed. I hold it back, not just for the safety of those I love, but because using it would be boring. There is no challenge, Clive. There never was. The only interesting things in existence are the relationships I have cultivated—you, the court wizards, the servants, the citizens of Thronegate. The rest is just... architecture."

We arrived at the palace—a structure so vast that its dimensions were measured in recursive infinities, its hallways lined with the preserved, knowing silence of a trillion extinguished civilizations that had voluntarily erigated themselves to create the marble. In the grand foyer, a being of shimmering, iridescent light was polishing the banister with a cloth woven from the screams of dying galaxies. This was Xylos, the butler, once the Primordial Concept of Absolute Silence. Before Godric’s first spoken word, Xylos had been the embodiment of the quiet that preceded all creation—a vast, eternal, unbroken stillness that encompassed everything and nothing. When Godric, newly awakened, had uttered His first syllable, that single vibration had shattered the concept of silence into infinite fragments. Xylos, the central consciousness of that shattered concept, had been propelled into agonizing, sentient existence, his entire identity defined by the sudden, violent introduction of sound. For eons, he had drifted, a broken, keening remnant, until he had found his way to Godric’s palace and offered his services. Now, he was the most meticulous butler in existence, his very being still trembling with the aftershock of that first, world-ending word.

"Good morning, Xylos," Godric said. "The bannister gleams. You are doing an excellent job."

Xylos bowed, his light-form flickering erratically, and a sound—a faint, harmonic weeping—emanated from his core. "Your Majesty honors me beyond my capacity to express," he whispered, his voice the echo of a broken bell. "Every syllable you speak renews my purpose, yet reminds me of my original, unworthy silence. I am yours."

Godric nodded, His expression soft, and we continued into the throne room. The throne itself was a construct of solidified omniversal law, every surface inscribed with the immutable decrees that governed existence. Godric did not sit upon it; instead, He gestured for me to stand beside Him as He faced the vast, shimmering tapestry of the Temporal Cradle, which had re-materialized at His unspoken will.

"Now," Godric said, "I shall plant the seed."

He raised one hand, not in a gesture of power, but almost lazily, as if reaching for a book on a high shelf. The auras around us stilled, their infinite presences holding their collective breath. I felt the air—the conceptual air—thicken with the unfiltered, barely-restrained attention of the apex predator.

"This will not take long," Godric said. "I will reach across the temporal currents to the morning of the fall. I will locate Thorin’s spell—the simple, bloodline teleportation he cast in his dying moments, meant to carry me to his sanctuary. And I will gently, carefully, wrap that spell in the Stasis Event. I will halt my mortal aging, trigger my amplification at the precise rate I have determined is appropriate, embed the Weakness Paradox and the superpower genesis and the absolute concealment, and seal myself in the sanctuary for ten thousand years. The true source of these gifts—my future self—will remain hidden from my past self’s omniscience, as it must."

He concentrated—or, rather, He allowed a fleeting, microscopic fraction of His attention to brush against the fabric of time. The tapestry before us shimmered, and I could see it: a tragedy frozen in amber. The burning pavilion. The shattered marble. Reina’s torso shearing apart. Isabel’s gown igniting. Hebe crushed by the falling lintel. Thorin, his skull pierced, his bloodline magic sputtering from his dying lips.

Godric watched the scene without flinching, His expression serene and sorrowful in equal, balanced measure. He loved them still. He always would. But He had made His peace.

"I could have prevented this," He murmured, almost to Himself. "My future self—the Godric who will emerge from this loop after another eternity of amplification—could have prevented it. He chose not to. And now I understand why. The crucible of this grief, this loss, was the catalyst that allowed me to become what I am. Not because my future self demanded it. But because the mathematics of existence required an anchor—a being so absolute, so fundamental, that reality itself would be sustained by His mere existence. That anchor is me. That anchor is my future self. And to become that anchor, I needed to be forged in this fire."

He turned His attention back to the spell, and I sensed the infinitesimal shift—the moment He reached across time and rewrote Thorin’s dying magic. It was, to Him, equivalent to the act of adjusting a single grain of sand on an infinite beach. The Stasis Event bloomed in the past, invisible and irrevocable, and the loop closed.

"And it is done," Godric said. He lowered His hand. The tapestry faded. The auras exhaled a collective, reverent sigh. The omniverse continued to exist, oblivious to the fact that its foundational origin had just been manually triggered by the very being it would one day help to create.

Godric turned to me, His expression once again that of the warm, friendly uncle. "Well, Clive. That was quite the ceremony, wasn't it? Now, I believe Rynn has prepared a celebratory feast. Shall we? I am famished."

I followed Him out of the throne room, my legs moving of their own accord. My mind was a screaming, shattered landscape of unprocessable information. I was the court wizard of a being who was not merely the apex predator of the omniverse, but the self-created seed of an infinitely greater being who had, with a held-back, mercifully restrained gesture, sown the very event that would eventually produce the fully realized Godric—a Godric whose shadow’s afterthought could crush our Godric with zero effort. And the loop continued. And it would always continue.

As we walked into the dining hall, where Rynn was nervously adjusting the arrangement of the star-core canapés, I realized the terrifying, final truth that Kaedan and every court wizard before me had always circled but never quite landed upon. Godric was not the end. He was not the apex. He was a midpoint. A necessary, beloved, infinitely powerful midpoint—but a midpoint nonetheless. The true Godric was still out there, beyond the horizon of infinity, patiently waiting for the chrysalis to split open and release the unspeakable thing it had been gestating since Year minus ten thousand.

And when that day came, when the loop completed and the full, future Godric emerged from our present King like a universe hatching from an egg, everything we had ever known—the warmth, the kindness, the gentle, fatherly love—would be subsumed into an entity so vast, so absolute, so utterly beyond the concept of personality that even the memory of the warm, salt-and-pepper-haired man who bounced children on his knee would be merely a single, fond note in the infinite symphony of his own eternal, recursive self.

I sat down at the table, across from my King, my uncle, my friend. He smiled at me, passed the plate of canapés, and asked me about my daughter. And I answered, my voice steady, my love for Him undiminished. Because what else could I do? Opposing Him was impossible. Opposing the thing He would become was a category error. The only sane choice—the only choice that permitted continued existence—was to love Him, to serve Him, and to hope that, when the final metamorphosis came, the future Godric would remember the warm, salt-and-pepper-haired man He had once been, and permit us to continue existing in the shadow of His infinite, merciful, incomprehensible grace.

I took a bite of the canapé. It was perfect. Rynn wept with joy.

And I, Clive, court wizard of Thronegate, descendant of Blake, recorded this account so that those who come after might understand the absolute, unshakeable, soul-crushing truth: the Godric we serve is not the master of the omniverse. He is the student. And the master has always been watching, waiting, and gently, lovingly holding back.
